schools.summary: Reads 'the Tishbite of Tishbe in Gilead' (LXX/Josephus); the name means 'whose God is Jehovah'; the oath marks him the servant standing to be sent, and this is not his first appearance (James 5:17).
Gill locates Gilead among Reuben, Gad, and half-Manasseh and derives the name Tishbite from Toshab or Thisbe.
Henry: Elijah drops out of the clouds like Melchizedek; a man of strong passions fit for rough service, as the Reformation needed a Luther; his shutting heaven is the power of prayer.
Clarke reports the fables of Elijah's angelic origin but grants they clash with James 5:17, and preserves Jarchi's legend of the taunt about Moses' curse.

outliers.note: Preserves the rabbinic (Jarchi) legend connecting the oath to Ahab's taunt about Joshua's and Moses' curses, and the conjecture that Elijah was an incarnate angel.

schools.summary: Gill (with the Targum): this is 'the word of prophecy,' proving that the 'my word' of v.1 was the Lord's word spoken by Elijah, not his own will.
Keil and Delitzsch: Elijah is hidden less for safety than to preclude all entreaties to lift the drought he had said would end only at his word.
The phrase 'before Jordan' is ambiguous (east of, or facing); Cherith was a ravine emptying into the Jordan, probably on the east near Gilead.
Clarke: the flight was necessary since Ahab would seek his life; the drought at the prophet's word proves God's power and the vanity of the idols.
Gill: he must turn from where he was, in danger from Ahab and Jezebel provoked by his prophecies.

debates.question: On which side of the Jordan did the brook Cherith lie?

sides.position: the eastern side, in or near the prophet's own Gilead
uncertain — the grammar allows either bank

schools.summary: Ellicott defends 'ravens' (with the ancient versions and Josephus) against 'Arabs/merchants,' calling this the second great epoch of Old Testament miracle after Moses and Joshua.
Clarke: Elijah is supplied by an especial providence, reserving fuller discussion for v.24.
Gill: the brook was to be his drink — God keeps a table in the very land he has dried up.

themes: miracle not to be explained away

schools.summary: Gill: Elijah journeyed eastward and hid at the appointed place, doing exactly 'according to the word of the Lord' — obedience without argument.

themes: according to the word of the Lord

schools.summary: Gill: the ravens brought ready-dressed food twice daily; the source (Ahab's or Jehoshaphat's table, or the faithful 7000) is left hidden, and the marvel is its constancy.
Clarke notes the Septuagint variant but keeps the Hebrew (bread and flesh at each meal), inferring this was the ordinary food of the people.

themes: bread and flesh morning and evening

schools.summary: Gill: 'after a while' is literally 'at the end of days,' perhaps marking the passing of a year at the brook.
Clarke: the brook dried because of the God-sent drought, the testimony against idolatry threatened in Deuteronomy 11.

Henry: God has various ways to provide; Zarephath, a Gentile city, makes Elijah the first prophet of the Gentiles (Luke 4:26), and a poor widow is chosen to shame the world's strength and Jezebel's malice.
Keil and Delitzsch: the word directs him to Zarephath of Sidon, to a widow God has commanded to sustain him.
Gill: a trial of faith, sent to dwell in Sidonian territory where Jezebel had interest.

Zarephath is the New Testament Sarepta (Luke 4:26), between Tyre and Sidon, identified with modern Surafend; 'which belongeth to Zidon' marks the providence that hid Elijah in his enemy's own country.
Gill distinguishes this Zarephath from another and judges it a Gentile city, citing Maundrell's ruins.
Clarke locates Sarepta near Sidon, present name Sarphan, following Maundrell's report.
Gill: Elijah arose and went the hundred miles from Cherith to Zarephath (per Bunting) — prompt obedience across a famine-struck and hostile land on nothing but the promise.
Gill: the widow makes no difficulty of fetching water, ready to show hospitality to a stranger she perceives to be a prophet.
Keil and Delitzsch: Elijah's requests for water and bread are a test to confirm whether this Gentile woman is the appointed protectress; her answer reveals a last handful of meal and a little oil.
Gill: her oath by the living God shows her a good woman who takes Elijah for a true prophet.
Ellicott: 'thy God' recognises Elijah's prophethood but implies no personal faith yet; the famine had reached Phoenicia (Menander) or was its reflex from Israel.
Clarke glosses the vessels — the 'barrel' an earthen jar for keeping meal, the 'cruse' a bottle (per Jarchi).

debates.question: Does 'the Lord thy God' show the widow's own faith or only courteous recognition?

sides.position: a good woman's true reverence for the living God and his prophet
recognition of Elijah's office, but no personal acknowledgment of God as yet

themes: as the Lord thy God liveth

schools.summary: Gill: 'Fear not' answers her dread of dying — the promise of life comes before the command.
Clarke: to give a stranger the last pittance that would keep her child alive was an extraordinary trial of faith; everything turns on 'make me a cake first.'
Gill: Elijah invokes 'the Lord God of Israel,' whom he perceived the widow already knew and trusted; the meal and oil are pledged to last till the rain returns.
Keil and Delitzsch: the promise was kept — she and her house ate many days 'according to the word of the Lord,' the miracle vindicating the spoken word.
Gill: she did as Elijah said, making his cake first — an act of great faith in the word of the Lord.
Ellicott: the miracle is doubly remarkable — God's higher laws meet homely needs, and multiplication is virtual creation, doing swiftly what providence does slowly.
Gill: a continued miracle of increase by God's mighty power, like the loaves and fishes multiplied as the disciples ate (John 6).
Clarke: she drew each day in confidence and was never disappointed, the increase coming in the spending — like the water made wine at Cana (John 2).
Gill: the sickness came after prophet and widow had lived a year or more on the miraculous provision.
Henry: the boy fed by miracle yet died; the loss is a thorn against pride; sharp afflictions come even in the way of duty, so we must rejoice with trembling.

Ellicott: 'man of God' marks growth from the 'thy God' of v.12; her cry, like Peter's 'depart from me,' springs from a deepened sense of sin. Keil and Delitzsch: the death was not for particular sin but to manifest God's works (John 9:3).
Clarke: she is conscious of some secret sin; adversity brings deep searchings of heart.
Gill: she spoke rashly, in passion and agony, forgetting the benefits received.

debates.question: Why was the child taken — as punishment for the widow's sin?

sides.position: as a stroke recalling and punishing her sin, as she herself feared
not for particular sin, but to manifest the works of God in her (John 9:3)

themes: what have I to do with thee

schools.summary: Gill: with the mother's leave Elijah tenderly takes the dead child from her bosom to his own bed in the loft.
Keil and Delitzsch: his cry 'hast Thou also brought evil' is no reproach but the deepest lamentation of living faith, sure God cannot leave the boy in death.

themes: faith that pours out the heart

schools.summary: Ellicott hears half-presumptuous impatience (as in 1 Kings 19); Keil and Delitzsch find no reproach but faith's lament, and explain the threefold stretching as bringing down God's power, not natural means (contrast Acts 20:10; 2 Kings 4:34).
Gill: he cried with great vehemence and importunity (so the Targum).

debates.question: Does Elijah's complaint carry reproach and impatience, or pure faith?

sides.position: a half-presumptuous impatience, hinting his own hard lot brought the trouble
no reproach at all, but the deepest lamentation of living faith

schools.summary: Ellicott: the stretching is no natural means but a healing 'virtue' in the prophet's person (as without measure in Christ, Luke 8), made conditional on prayer.
Gill: Elijah measured himself on the child in fervent affection, to stir his prayer and as if to infuse his own life.
Clarke: he sought to give natural warmth to dispose the body to receive the spirit, and reads 'let his soul come' as proof of an immortal soul existing apart from the body.

debates.question: What did the threefold stretching on the child signify?

sides.position: a natural means — warmth to dispose the body to receive the returning spirit
a healing virtue in the prophet's person, effectual only through prayer, not natural means

themes: let this child's soul come again

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: in raising the boy Elijah shows himself the forerunner of Him who raises all the dead, and the Gentile woman's faith is mightily strengthened.
Gill: the Lord heard Elijah's prayer and answered it — the miracle rests wholly on that hearing.
Clarke: 'the soul came into the midst of him' proves an immortal, immaterial spirit that can exist apart from the body, like a spring at the center of a machine.
Gill: Elijah brings the living child down from the loft to the lower house and gives him to his mother with 'See, thy son liveth.'
Keil and Delitzsch: the restoration mightily strengthened the Gentile woman's faith, so that she perceived the word in his mouth to be truth.
Ellicott: her faith rises by stages — 'the Lord thy God' (v.14), then God (v.18), now the word as truth — expressing conversion, like the nobleman at Capernaum (John 4:53).
Gill: taken for a man of God at first, confirmed by the meal and oil, staggered by the death, she is now fully assured by the resurrection.
Clarke: through a little suffering came self-examination, God's power, and conviction of the word's truth; then he reopens v.4, arguing the feeders were men (of Orbo/Arabians), not ravens.

debates.question: Who fed Elijah at the brook — ravens or men?

sides.position: literal ravens, with the ancient versions and Josephus
men — merchants, Arabians, or townsmen of Orbo near Beth-shan

themes: now I know thou art a man of God
